descriptionSummary: Trust Horizon (Trust Variation) Bill. Dana Kirkpatrick. Trust Horizon

chunks.text: of the bill that are being presented to broaden the funding from energy related to charitable purposes. And today, the trust can only fund energy related purposes or projects. This bill would enable the trust to support any charitable purpose that benefits the district community, in addition to continuing to support energy related purposes. And I think it's worth noting that they would then have to determine what their eligibility criteria looks like, and what their vision for the region was, and how their grant funding would

help to support that. And the second purpose, the bill will alter the trust deed so it can be updated in the future with safeguards, which allows for future flexibility. Instead of needing another act of Parliament for future trust deed updates, the bill allows future amendments with strong safeguards, and trustees would be able to amend terms only where it would be in the trust's best interest to further the charitable purpose.

I think it's worth understanding a little bit about the Eastern Bay of Plenty community. Population size, 37,000. Māori, the share of the Māori population there, 56%, large Māori community. High area of deprivation. Average household income, well below the New Zealand average. The benefits received, very high in terms of comparison with the New Zealand average. Home ownership, low.

Rent affordability pressure, very high. NCEA level two at age 18, well below average. And the NEATS rate, very high. School leavers with no qualification, well above the national average. The community is in need of support in ways that it can deliver better projects and better community deliverables in so many different areas in the voluntary and community organization space.

In the Eastern Bay of Plenty, there's only a certain number of energy related purposes available to support within the district, which has led to 30 years of static grant distribution despite population growth. By the end of this year, the trust will have distributed about $55 million in energy related purposes, and the trust estimates this change could mean an additional 1 million to 2 million more per year available for the community in the Eastern Bay of Plenty, which supports community and sporting organizations, and the many volunteers who keep the public sector and the

not-for-profit organizations going. At the same time, as I said, the community wellbeing goes well beyond energy, and the limitation on making grants only for energy related purposes restricts the trust's ability to respond to changing community needs or to support charities, societies, and other worthy groups that do not meet this definition. With the present limitation on energy related purposes, the trust has been able to grant about $900 per person in its district over the past 30 years. And by contrast, the neighboring

Rototuna Trust, which has broader charitable purposes, has been able to grant about $2,000 per person in its district over the same period. In considering the need for change, the trustees have traversed many conversations, and I think it worth covering off some of those for the benefit today. As was statutorily required, the trust consulted widely, including conducting a poll, of which 3,625 people responded to, and 80.7% of those supported the changes.

Those who supported the bill supported a wider range of social, educational, cultural, and environmental initiatives to include a wider variety of community projects and groups. This expansion is seen as a way to strengthen healthy, vibrant, and innovative communities, by providing grants that address diverse needs. For example, youth initiatives, education, wellbeing, community partnerships, and support for Māori and rural communities were frequently highlighted as important areas for funding.

Of those who opposed the changes, many felt it might result in higher power prices for consumers, but this often comes from confusion about the difference between the trust and the electricity distribution company, Horizon Energy. Related to this, the trustees considered a variation of the energy related purposes under Part 3 of the Charitable Trusts Act, so they looked to see if that was an easier way to make this change. However, the ability to vary charitable purposes under the Charitable Trusts Act is limited, and the trustees do not want a narrow change to the energy

related purposes. They desire broader charitable purposes, which they can then define through their funding criteria and eligibility, to allow them to do more and fund more in the community. The trustees explored a consumer distribution model, but Trust Horizon is quite different to most other energy trusts in that it is a charitable trust, so in distributing funds, it has to meet both the trustee purpose and be charitable in law. Providing a small, regular payment to all electricity users, as some consumer trusts do around the country,

is not a charitable activity and so would not qualify. The trust instead granted up to $2,000 to support charitable causes with energy bills and co-funded the EECA Warmer Kiwi Homes initiatives to make insulation and heating free for anyone that was eligible. This equated to, on average, $1,000 for insulation and $700 for heating. The trust has been integral in insulating more than 50% of all homes in the district through its insulation contribution, creating energy savings and healthy homes for past, current, and future generations.

And it has a community energy contribution grant each year for up to $2,500 per eligible organization, to assist with the impact of power prices. It provides low interest lending for organizations wanting to install solar and has deployed over $1 million in funding to date for this purpose. This includes lots of different equipment, such as electric lawnmowers, rollers, electric mobility vans, school EV chargers, community bike chargers, and others.

It supports local groups with the energy transition funding projects at around 1 to $2 million in granting a year. And still, the funds accumulate due to the narrow nature of the trust. So the trustees have agreed that a wider charitable purpose would be the most efficient way for the trust to assist with that need, and a private bill is the only way to achieve that goal.

The trust has already committed letters of support to us, which I have seen, for the bill from the Energy Distribution Limited, Horizon Energy Distribution Limited, the settler of the trust, the Bay Trust, Bay of Plenty Regional Council, Kawerau District Council, Opotiki District Council, Whakatane District Council, Te Runanga o Ngati Awa, Ngati Manua, Ngati Fare, TECT, the Rotorua Trust, and Philanthropy New Zealand.Trust Horizon frames its purpose as creating transformational change and bringing pride and prosperity to the region with a

focus on long-term tangible benefits. The trust's intention is to unlock transformational change by enabling more funding that positively impacts communities and supports a wider range of people in the community, projects, and groups across Whakatane, Kawerau, Opotiki, and Kaingaroa Village. In their words, the long-term vision is a thriving Eastern Bay, where our funding can respond to what communities need most over time, and where the trust can invest for durable outcomes rather than being locked into a narrow 1990s

definition of energy-related purposes. So, I could go on for a long time. But we are at time, and- And I think that's enough from me Thank you very much for that very comprehensive submission. I appreciate it. It's my pleasure, and I am very proud to be presenting the trust and the bill and the community in this process.
Thank you. Thank you. Thank you for the work that you do. Okay. Right. Our next speaker, or next submitter, is Trust Horizon, and I believe Derek Caldwell, the chief executive, is presenting online.

Or in person? In person. Online. I'm one of the lawyers assisting Oh, okay. That's all right. Good morning, Derek, and welcome. We have 15 minutes with you this morning. So if you wanted to introduce yourselves, and then we just go through, and if you want to leave any time for questions, please feel free to do so. But the time is yours.

Good morning. Thank you very much. Tena koutou katoa. Thank you all for taking the time to hear submissions today on Trust Horizon's variation bill, and in particular to our local MP and Deputy Chair of the committee, Dana Kirkpatrick, who's been integral to getting us to this point in the process. Ko Derek Caldwell ahau. My name is Derek Caldwell, and I'm the CEO of Trust Horizon. I'm joined today by the trust chair, David Glover.

And in the room with you is Jonathan Orpin-Dowd from Stout Street Chambers, who's been our legal counsel, along with Sharp Tudhope. It's our pleasure to be able to present our submission to you today on behalf of Trust Horizon, a registered charitable trust serving the Eastern Bay of Plenty for over 30 years.

To make the best use of the time, I'll highlight a few key areas from our submission and leave plenty of time for questions. At its core, this bill is about enabling Trust Horizon to continue fulfilling its charitable mission of providing transformational investing into our community well into the future by unlocking funding beyond energy-related purposes to ensure our governing deed reflects and is responsive to current and future community needs.

Trust Horizon was established in '94 as part of the electricity sector reforms under the Energy Companies Act. Our primary purpose is to grant funds back into the local communities, with over 50 million granted over our 30 plus years of operation. One of our proudest achievements has been early and sustained investment into home insulation, alongside the government, helping to insulate tens of thousands of homes, reduce energy poverty, and improve public health outcomes.

The trust now holds assets of over 200 million, making it a significant and enduring philanthropic institution in one of New Zealand's most socioeconomically deprived areas. The trust has a vision of enabling pride, prosperity, and people through our funding. In order to be able to make grants to the community, we hold a range of investments, our largest investment being in the Horizon Energy Group, which is an independently governed and run company that evolved from the original setter of the trust.

Despite our scale and capacity, Trust Horizon faces a fundamental constraint. Our trust deed limits us to funding energy-related purposes only. Trust Horizon is now the only energy trust in New Zealand that remains exclusively restricted in this way. That's right. While energy remains critical, especially for households facing hardship, there is now a natural ceiling on how many genuinely charitable energy-specific projects exist within our district.

As a result, annual grant levels have remained largely static until very recently, and the trust capital has continued to grow. Our ability to respond to broader community need has been artificially restricted. The issue we face is not a lack of demand, but eligible scope for organizations to apply for funding.

The Eastern Bay of Plenty is highly deprived, and the community need is obvious. Trustees are doing what they can, with the vast majority of applications we receive being funded. Yet when we look across to our peers and neighboring communities, we know that we can be doing much more. The Eastern Bay of Plenty includes some of the most deprived communities in the country, and we all know that community wellbeing extends well beyond energy purposes alone into areas such as housing, health, education, the environment, and social cohesion.

By comparison, neighboring charitable trusts with broader purposes have been able to deliver significantly greater per person funding over the same period. The trustees believe, strongly and unanimously, that the trust has the capacity, and indeed the obligation, to do more. The bill proposes making two targeted and principled changes.

Firstly, it broadens the trust purposes from being limited to energy-related purposes to supporting any charitable purpose recognized in New Zealand law, which allows us to continue to fund energy-related initiatives as well as other activities. This does not alter our charitable status or allow private benefit or change how or in what we invest funds to generate returns for our granting.

And importantly, it does not diminish our energy-related heritage.It simply allows trustees to respond appropriately to real and evolving needs of our communities. Secondly, the bill introduces a sensible mechanism for future trust deed updates. Rather than requiring another private act of Parliament, any future changes would be assessed by trustees as being in the trust's best interests, further its charitable purposes, and require High Court approval.

This provides flexibility while maintaining strong and independent safeguards. Why do we need to come to Parliament to do this? Over the trust's history, it has explored every available alternative and found no other way to achieve this change. The courts cannot make the change under existing trust law, and the Charitable Trust Act expressly prevents the type of purpose expansion proposed here.

Only Parliament has the authority to modernize the trust's purposes in this way. Importantly, had the trust been set up slightly differently in '94, these broader purposes could have existed from the outset, as they do for many comparable trusts. This bill seeks to correct this historic anomaly.

The trustees have not taken preparing a bill to Parliament lightly and have sought community-wide mandate to do so. Our region-wide community poll showed over 80% support for both proposed changes, and we have received strong written support from local councils, iwi and hapū, fellow charitable trusts, community organizations, and our settler.

This is a well-tested, community-endorsed proposal. In closing, this bill is not about expanding power. It is about unlocking public benefit for community good. It will allow the trust to reset and scale its community funding in proportion with the equity it holds on behalf of the community. It modernizes a 30-year-old trust deed while preserving charitable integrity and strong oversight. Most importantly, it allows Trust Horizon to better serve the people of the Eastern Bay of Plenty, today and for generations to come.

We really appreciate the committee's consideration of this matter, and we respectfully ask the committee to recommend the bill to proceed. Thank you, and we welcome any questions you may have. Thank you very much. We do have a few questions. I'll start with Lawrence. Thank you, Mr. Chair, and thank you for joining us, and thank you to your local MP for bringing this bill.

I just have, I guess, one or maybe a supplementary question. So I'm noticing, I'm noting that it says the trust now has more than $200 million in assets. What proportion of that, either by proportional amount currently, is going towards the existing purpose, which is energy-related objectives? And that's been the first part of the question, and the second part is, by unlocking it to charitable purpose, what projections do you have in terms of the proportion of that $200 million will be going towards charitable purposes, or if you have amount figure for that as well?

Yeah. So, in our trust submission, it's about on page five and six. We've presented some graphs showing the percentages of our equity. So at the moment, it sits at around anywhere between 1% and 1.5% of our equity goes towards energy-related purposes.

So that, in broad terms, that's about $3.5 million per annum. Other charitable trusts, they can achieve 3% to 4.5%. We think certainly by unlocking the charitable purposes in the short to medium term, it'll unlock a further $1 to $2 million per annum into the community.

Thank you. Longer term, hopefully, we can achieve similar levels that other charitable trusts do. Next question, Helen. Hi. First of all, I was born in Kawerau, so I have a stake in this game. I know how important money is there, in terms of unlocking the potential and the levels of deprivation.

What I want to know is, first of all, it sounds like you've tried to saturate the need for energy efficiency, and I'd love to know what you've seen as a result in terms of particularly health, and then I'd like to know what kinds of projects are likely. We haven't had a specific example, so what kinds of projects do you hope to fund in a community like Kawerau?

Yeah. So, in terms of the projects we'd like to fund, the energy-related restriction really limits our ability to fund operational granting. So, salaries and other operational expenses that really make a huge difference to charities and nonprofits because that allows them to sustain a kaupapa for a longer period of time and have much more impact in the community.

So that's really an area that we'd like to unlock with this change. Sorry, the first part of the question was about the impact of our insulation activities over the years and the health benefits. Of what you've done in the energy space. It sounds like you've done what you can in the energy space, and it's not something that we've seen across the country.

And I know that insulation, for example, makes a huge difference to things like rheumatic fever, and health outcomes, in general, if you have warm, dry homes. I'd just love to know what you've seen in that community as a result of your work. Yeah. So there have been some studies, one sort of in the early 2000s, around the health impacts, but there haven't, as far as I'm aware, haven't sort of been any follow-up-Studies specifically on the Eastern Bay of Plenty community.

Unfortunately, rheumatic fever, respiratory illness is still a real problem in our communities, despite all the investment that has gone into insulating homes. Insulation is, as you'll be aware, sort of only one part of the equation. You also need to heat the homes. And so, Warmer Kiwi Homes, moving into the home heating space in the last couple of years, hopefully over time we'll see that change have a real impact in terms of health outcomes for our community.

Thank you. And we've been sitting alongside Warmer Kiwi Homes to make that free for those who are eligible in our community. Next question, Jamie. Thank you for the submission. I'm quite interested in your definition of charitable purposes, and I'm wondering where that definition has come from, and the part mostly is where you got elsewhere in New Zealand. And I understand that obviously you've got then the directly or indirectly benefiting the community.

So could you give me some where the definitions come from and how that's been landed on? And then I guess from this committee's point of view, how much we focus on that definition. Jonathan, are you able to comment on that? Sure. So the general definition of charitable purposes just picks up the definition that exists under existing charitable law.

So it allows the trustees to take projects that will be charitable and decide what the highest and best use is. In terms of the language about directly or indirectly in other parts of New Zealand, the thinking is that it's a small community, so there might be, for example, a project that's taking place, like a hospital, that's going to be built outside the district but which will serve people in the district.

And that by making a contribution, then they can provide better access for people in the district through maybe busing, getting them into the services. But you'll never provide all the social services that you'd want for a community of people in a district of that size. So by allowing the trustees to contribute to things outside the district, as long as they're satisfied it's going to benefit the community, then you've unlocked the potential to do that. It would obviously be a matter of trustee discretion, wouldn't expect them to fund services that have got no connection to the district.

But if you've got something, say, that's in the largest metropolitan area outside the district, so Tauranga, and that's going to serve X percentage of the community, and you want them to have those services, realistically, the only way is to allow that outside as long as there's a connection back in. So that's why the language about the connection is there, and then it will be a matter for the trustees to satisfy themselves in the ordinary way that making that particular grant would actually serve their community, even though geographically,

the funds might be going outside the district. Does that make sense? It does, yeah. Can I just... A second question was the trustees, obviously it's been energy-related purposes. So will there be a hierarchy between the energy-related purpose or projects will have higher priority over the charitable purposes? How will you then decide which projects take priority considering where you've been in the past?

So, we are in the process on the basis that hopefully this bill will proceed, of exploring how we're going to have to recalibrate our grant decision-making, because we will have a wider world to consider. But one thing all the trustees are very unanimous about is we are not walking away from our energy-related purpose history.

All those things, the insulation still needs to continue. All the things that we're currently doing are doing good things, we believe. So it's part of our history, but it's also part of our future priority. Last question, Maureen. Thanks very much. I'm not sure who to direct this at, but I'd just like to refer to the High Court's comment in your submission around the declining of the trust's application in 2006, which says that under the trustee, the central terms of the trust were intended to remain constant during its natural life.

Yet this proposed bill is a massive departure from that, and it's a very broad departure. So did you consider a more narrow rationale or a more narrowing of the purpose rather than such a broad constitution? Do you want me to take that, Derek?

Yeah, if you could, Jonathan. Yeah, so, this well predates my involvement in the matter, but I understand that it's been an issue for the trustees for some time about how they can modernize and update the trust deed, including its purposes. As members will probably be aware, there's nothing that prevents you, when you settle a trust, having a power to change the terms of the trust, and lots of other community trusts that were set up at the time did that, and so they don't have to come before you and take up your valuable time.

So the trustees in 2006 tried to effectively achieve a similar aim by resettling assets onto a new trust, which would have broader purposes than the current trust. And what the High Court said is, and this just relates to general trust principles- Mm.... that the resettlement wouldn't be valid because the purpose of the resettlement was effectively to change the terms of the trust deed, which there wasn't a power in the trust deed to do. There could've been, but they hadn't put one in.

So what the High Court is saying is you can't do this under your existing trust deed and in light of general trust law principles. But of course, the reason that we have Parliament in relation to the charitable space is that when that's not possible, Parliament can approve broader changes, which is why we've come to you. In terms of the second part of the question, as I understand it, and Derek and the trustees can comment, we accept it would be possible to achieve a narrower variation through the Charitable Trusts Act 1957. But those variations

have to hew as closely as possible to the original purpose of the deed, which means that you could expand the district maybe, or maybe have adjacent purposes to energy-related purposes, but you couldn't open up the universe of charitable purposes. And so the thinking is what is best from a public policy point of view, and I guess that's really what the trustees are asking the committee to decide and endorse, is to use the funds where they can be best used, where they've got the highest and best use, and the only way to do that is to allow

the universe of charitable purposes. And then trustees can consider the applications that come before them, and if they're in some way related to tackling rheumatic fever or energy-related purposes that are not currently covered but are wider than that, then they can say, "Well, what's the business case for these things?" And choose the best one. If they go down the Charitable Trusts 1957 process, they'll just have to hew as closely as possible to what's there, even though there might be much better projects out there in terms of where you'd get best value for investment.

Does that make sense? It does make sense, but it doesn't satisfy my inquiry- Sure... because the The charitable purpose is related to energy projects. Yes. And I still can't see how those energy-related projects are not saturated yet. I know we heard from Dana Kirkpatrick that they have exhausted them.

But when you look at improvements to the supply of electricity, inadequate or unreliable supply issues, I can't see that those opportunities haven't yet been exhausted. Or even if there is a, I'll call it a problem or a challenge with too much cash, maybe the distribution that they're getting could be reallocated into reducing the lines charges for everybody, which would serve everybody. But what we're expecting the trust to do is then pick winners in their community.

And you ask anybody if they want more cash, of course, they're going to say yes. So I'm not surprised that the community is behind this. But in terms of why the trust was set up in the first place and the charitable purpose that was established for energy-related purposes, I'm not sure yet that the trust has saturated all their ability to exhaust that cash- Sure... in those projects, even the research and development opportunities that could exist.

So there's probably a legal answer and a factual answer to that, and perhaps I can help with the legal answer and then leave the trustees to talk about the factual answer. But part of the legal answer is that although they are energy-related purposes, and you mentioned, for example, reducing distribution costs for consumers, they still have to be charitable. And so, granting that would just go directly to individual consumers as some of the other energy-related trusts that were set up wouldn't be charitable because it's providing

individual pecuniary benefits to people. And the law of charity says that that's not generally charitable. And so even within that scope of energy-related purposes, being charitable imposes limitations on what you can do. And then I think the sort of factual answer, which Derek can pick up on, is I don't think anyone's saying that there won't be more energy-related granting to do going forward, but just that if you look at the number of applications that are coming in, they're approving, I think 80, 90% of them each year.

But that's still leading to equity building up because there's not enough applications coming in that they can grant that come within the terms of it. So the idea is that if you make this change, they can continue doing that granting work that they're doing around their 1%. But in terms of moving up to comparable levels where they wouldn't be running down the capital and would still be continuing to grow but putting more money into the community, they can do that through other projects because they're just not getting the applications at the moment that fit within the terms.

Mm-hmm. Thank you. We are well over time, but I see there are more questions, but we can put through ones afterwards. But look, I-- well, thank- And if you've got more questions, we're happy to answer them in writing. Give some more detail. Yeah, can do that. Be very happy to do that. Thank you very much. No, thank you all for presenting to the committee this morning. Appreciate it. Thank you very much. Thank you. Thank you. Our next submitter is Bay Trusts, Bay of Plenty Community Trust, and we have Alison Rhodes, the Chief Executive.
I believe it's online.

Good morning, Alison. Welcome to the committee. So we have 10 minutes with you this morning if you want to introduce yourselves, and then... Sorry, we have 15 minutes with you this morning. No, 10, sorry. Just double-check my calculation. No, it is 10 minutes. And so the time is yours, and if you want to leave time for questions, please feel free to do so.

Kia ora. Just checking you can hear us fine. Yeah, we can. No, thank you. Awesome. Kia ora koutou. Ko RT, Chief Executive of Bay Trust. Ko Alistair Rhodes toku ingoa. Nau reira, tena koutou katoa. Look, I'm really privileged to be a CEO of Bay Trust and presenting on behalf of Bay Trust our submission to the select committee today, where we'll be strongly endorsing the Trust Horizon Variation Bill.

Also want to introduce some colleagues. To my left is Sam Cummins. So Sam Cummins is Bay Trust's Community Investment and Policy Manager. And I think I'd argue, say Bay-- Sam has got probably one of the most richest experience of the not-for-profit and charitable sector down in the Eastern Bay of Plenty because we offer a broad range of funding down into that kind of sector.

Look, I know how busy the select committee is, and I assume you've actually received and, I'll take this, read our written submission. So I don't want to go through that written submission in detail. I just want to highlight a few key points and make sure we leave plenty of time at the end of this session for question and answers, which is probably going to be the most valuable as well. In terms of context, Bay Trust is a community trust, so we've been around for 35-odd years.

We were set up to own the shares of Trust Bank, and in 1996, we sold those shares, $89 million, to Westpac and created a diversified investment fund, which is worth today around $290 million. Annually, we grant around 8 to 9 million out to the Bay of Plenty kind of communities, and about 2 million of that is granted into the Eastern Bay of Plenty communities each year as well.

The other thing that's unique about Bay Trust, we're one of 12 community trusts that operate under the Community Trust legislation. And under that legislation, we have a very broad mandate or purpose, which I think is really important to note. So under the act, it says that we're there to grant anything in terms of charitable, cultural, philanthropic, recreational, or any other purpose. So really broad purpose in terms of actually what we do and what we grant out to in the community.

And that's important because over the last 35 years, our community needs have changed significantly. What we used to grant 35 years ago is not such an important need as what we grant now. An example of that is social media over the last few years in terms of some of the harm caused, which we now grant into that area, which simply did not exist 35 years ago. And that's why having a very broad purpose actually makes a lot of sense for a philanthropic foundation like ourselves.As far as we can recall, this is the first time in

Bay Trust's 35 years that we've ever presented to a select committee, and this does illustrate the importance we hold in terms of this kind of process and in terms of our support for this Trust Horizon Variation bill. We are one of the few significant philanthropic funders down in the Eastern Bay of Plenty, and we have direct oversight in terms of the unmet need in that charitable and the not-for-profit sector down there.

Pretty much for every $2 to $3 requested of funding, we might be able to provide grant funding of around $1, so there's a significant unmet need. And when we look at those applications going through, most of them are very, very impactful. We'd like to fund a lot more down into that region, but we simply do not have sufficient funding to do that.

In particular, areas we noticed that would need sufficient more funding down into the Eastern Bay of Plenty, youth development, whanau support, aged care, food security, budgeting services, and environmental services are all crying out for further funding down into that region. We also, every year over the last 10 years, we've surveyed our Eastern Bay of Plenty not-for-profit sector and our communities down there to understand what their actual needs are.

And it's very clear when we get those survey responses back that they are desperately crying out for more funding in terms of early operational cost funding, that backbone funding in terms of those aforementioned kind of services. The Eastern Bay of Plenty region is one of our most deprived regions across New Zealand. You think of districts like Ōpōtiki and Kawerau, they always rank pretty much at the top of our deprivation indexes.

So when we talk about the Eastern Bay of Plenty, we're talking about some of our most disadvantaged communities in the country. At present, as you've previously discussed, Trust Horizon is constrained to energy-related purposes. That worked reasonably well at the start, but as our community's needs do change, for a philanthropic organization to have the most impact, being able to have some flexibility in terms of that purpose and to respond to changing needs over time, we think is incredibly important.

So we do consider amending the trust deed and extending its charitable purpose as both appropriate and necessary, and provides the flexibility to meet those community needs as they change and adapt over time. We've seen the impact of this firsthand in our other regions. For example, in the Western Bay of Plenty, TECT, one of our other big funders down here, clarified and changed and expanded their purposes a few years ago, and we've seen the positive impact that's made to our communities.

We also recognize Trust Horizon has a strong record of governance and a long-standing commitment to community benefit, and we are confident with an expanded mandate, Trust Horizon would continue to operate with integrity and increase its positive impact and make much more of a meaningful impact in the Eastern Bay of Plenty communities.

So accordingly, Bay Trust, on behalf of our board, we strongly support the Trust Horizon Variation bill, and we really encourage the select committee to support and progress the proposed changes. And so both Sam and I, as I said, I've tried to keep this short, are available to ask any kind of questions the select committee has of us. Oh, beautiful.

Great. Thank you. Lawrence. Thank you, Chair. Thank you for your submission. Looking at your submission, and just in terms of what the Horizon Trust mentioned as well, I have one question, I guess for trust in general. What's the application process like to apply for funding, and is that application process accessible?

Do you do any education campaigns as well on how to apply? Yeah. Kia ora, Lawrence. We have regular community engagement, so we're in the community often where we run hui workshops on how to apply. We're always more than willing to help applicants, and our application process is online. So go on our website and follow that through, and it takes around 90 minutes for the average application, so that's fairly short.

The intention is to be as accessible as possible for the community. And can I check, and this is also something that just occurred to me now. In one of the previous submissions, it talks about the high percentage of Maori populations in East Coast. Are applications allowed to be bilingual?

Yes. Yeah. Okay. And we have a specific Maori iwi relationship manager who can facilitate that if it needs to be. Yeah. That's really cool. Thank you. Thank you. Maureen. Yes. I just have a question around the capital. So the accumulation of capital, rather than a shift in energy needs or research and development or whatever, can you explain why you think that would be justification for a departure from the charitable purpose, the existing charitable purpose?

Is that a question for us, Maureen, or is that a question for Trust Horizon? Oh, well, Trust Horizon have gone, but-... in terms of- Sorry.
